Hey guys, welcome back. This is going to be a walkthrough of the module 6 workbook. So we just saw the main lesson from the sales sequence and just as a reminder, this is the sequence that occurs from the moment we open up cart until 48 hours before the close. This is the part of your launch that will usually represent the greatest lull. But if engineered correctly, like we're going to do with the theme plates below, they could still have the power to really bring in those steady conversions. So that sales don't take that dramatic nosedive between your initial launch and your close, as well as set up the conversion for the closing sequence just by reinforcing and recontextualizing the beliefs you instilled during the pre-launch. So now what I mean by recontextualizing is all those beliefs that you reframe, that you delivered into pre-launch are now contextualized with your product now present. So that's the job of the sales sequence to integrate, throw your product into that mixing bowl and make sure that it blends in well with all the beliefs you've already created for them. And that's kind of a good little metaphor and creates a tasty little treat for your prospect. So what we're not doing here is bringing new sales arguments into the mix. This is only going to dilute all that important work you've already done and overwhelm your prospect into a state of analysis paralysis. And you also want to avoid bringing time urgency into play too early and definitely not before 48 hours. That's why we have the distinction between the sales sequence and the closing sequence. Because if you start talking about just three days left, 72 hours left, whatever it is, it actually has that counter effect and makes your prospect feel like they have time to put off the decision. They're like, great, I'll think about this in three days. And not only are they procrastinating, but you're losing that momentum that you've generated. And that's really the most crucial part here is just keeping that foot on the accelerator and not letting them off the hook. So with all that said, here is what we are coaching. Once again, we're coaching identification with being a buyer, letting your prospect see themselves as a buyer. And we're selling a recommitment. We're closing that consistency loop we set up earlier during the confirmation stage. And we're revealing risk. So we're not reversing it yet, but we're doing the risk revealer, which gets our prospect extremely intimate with the stakes and the cost of going backwards. That's what keeps the momentum going. And then we are reinforcing the beliefs, reframes, outcomes, the slaughter cows and those ah-has using proof. You know, our offer is now here. People have used it. We have some testimonials. We have some credibility. That's part of how we recontextualize. So with all that said, we are going to dive into some questions from our 47 Questions Workbook. As always, if you've done them before, this is your chance to review them and really kind of add maybe some more insights that you've generated since that initial time you spend with the workbook. But here we go. Parallel realities divergent roads. This is a big thing that's going to really help you kind of reveal that risk, you know, the cost of going backwards if they don't buy your product, as well as help create safety in the outcome. Safety in the outcome comes from predictability, from seeing what it will look like and feeling at ease with that future. So the point is to make this extremely realistic. Don't use too much hyperbole when detailing these parallel realities. Be very real. Be very grounded. If parts of the process are going to suck and not be seamless, say they're going to suck and not be seamless because when you're honest about that, that gives credibility that when you are talking about the positive outcomes and the positive results, that that's true. So you want to be specific here. You also want to be real and grounded by all means. Talk about the positive outcomes that they're going to experience in those time frames, but ground it to something that's extremely realistic for them and also acknowledge parts of it that may not be so much fun. So in vivid detail, what does success look like? Three days from now, three weeks, three months, 12 months, and then we're going to reverse it. If they don't buy your product, what's it going to look like in three days, three weeks, three months, and 12 months? And a few other additional questions. What beliefs and micro commitments have they agreed to that you can call them out and stay consistent with? What are the questions a perfect buyer would ask or have asked before saying yes? And this goes beyond just kind of like that general Q&A. What are those questions that people are asking before they buy? Because you don't want to just have like a general FAQ where people have asked this, what have buyers asked? Because that's what's going to really be help current prospects identify with buyers. So yeah, I have that question on my mind. Oh, that person had it too and they became a buyer. So it creates that alignment. What case studies, stories, proof elements, can you link the beliefs and reframes too? So this is where you recontextualize it. Place those beliefs in context with what your offer will deliver or has been proven to deliver based on case studies or testimonials. What midcard bonus can you offer for 24 hours that would either set them up for that next thing after completing your program? So what this does is it presupposes A, that they bought and B, that they've achieved the success and the outcomes that your course is promising and now they're ready for this next thing, this graduation gift if you will. But you can also have a midcard bonus that helps them achieve an exciting outcome that's presented in your case study or at least complements your core program in helping get that specific outcome that you outlined. What's the real hyper specific cost of going backwards? So that's the risk revealer. You know, if I don't learn how to play electric guitar or acoustic guitar, sorry, what's that cost of going backwards? I'm still going to go on my annual camping trip. There's still going to be a bunch of buddies around the fire and I'm still going to be wishing that I was the one getting all that attention. You know, playing Green Day's Time of Your Life while everyone else just kind of soaks it in and looks upon this guitar playing God with envy, you know? And that's to me that moment of highest tension and the cost of going backwards will usually relate with that. It will usually mean continuing to experience that moment of highest tension. We spoke about earlier and what's that big benefit of moving forward? So this is the future pace. We did the exercise just a little earlier where we gave different timeframes for that. So that's a big core piece of the sales sequence. And what are those three biggest initial objections that eventual buyers have? And how can you frame that skepticism as a positive trait, you know, of the trait of someone who eventually invests? I want you to invite skepticism from your prospects, you know, and not frame skepticism as a characteristic or an action of someone who is not a buyer. I want you to instead see how you can include skepticism and concern and questions and all these things as someone who is a buyer, someone who, you know, really, really wants to get that result, but they've been maybe burned before and they just don't have that level of believability anymore. So within every skeptic, there is someone lurking within that wants to overcome it. No one wants to remain skeptical perpetually. So skepticism isn't a bad thing. And how can you include that as an aspect of a buyer as a positive trait? So we are going to show you how to work these elements in in just a second. We're going to look at a theme plate called the upgraded FAQ, which is really going to help you ground some of these elements that we're coaching. So take a breather, fill out these questions because they are going to serve you and make the next steps in this lesson a lot more easier if you already have that in front of you. So take some time to do that right now and meet me in theme plate 11 when you're ready. So I'll see you in a bit.
